claim: The text-only section's elevated entropy (3.9016 bits, highest of all sections) and the recipes section's second-highest entropy (3.8586 bits) reflect unenciphered or lightly enciphered running prose in the underlying natural language, while the diagrammatic sections (zodiac 3.7149, astronomical 3.7471, pharmaceutical 3.7772) reflect a more heavily enciphered or formulaic register â€” meaning entropy difference between sections correlates with cipher strength, not genre.
evidence_for: Section entropy spans 0.19 bits (3.7149 to 3.9016), which is large enough to suggest qualitative differences in encoding rather than mere genre effects
Text-only section (7 folios, 2,349 words) has no diagrams, suggesting if the scribe used a cipher, they may have applied it less rigorously to continuous prose
Recipes section (25 folios, 11,611 words) is the largest section and has the second-highest entropy, consistent with extensive prose where cipher slippage is more likely
Diagrammatic sections (zodiac, astronomical, pharmaceutical) require labeling short, repeated terms â€” contexts where a skilled encipherer applies cipher most consistently
evidence_against: Genre differences alone could explain entropy variation: prose naturally has higher per-glyph entropy than labels and formulaic lists
The 0.19-bit entropy range across sections is within the range produced by genre differences in plaintext medieval Latin (prose vs. lists)
Text-only section being unenciphered would imply the scribe intentionally left 7 folios in plaintext, an implausible operational security failure
test: For each section, compute the rank-frequency slope (Zipf exponent) separately. If cipher-strength variation drives entropy, sections with lower entropy should show steeper Zipf slopes (more concentrated frequency mass) than high-entropy sections. Additionally, compute the ratio of top-10-word frequency mass to total word count per section and correlate this ratio with section entropy.
test_metric: Pearson correlation between per-section entropy and per-section Zipf exponent; Pearson correlation between per-section entropy and per-section top-10 word frequency mass ratio
test_threshold: Negative correlation (r < -0.7) between entropy and Zipf slope would support cipher-strength hypothesis; correlation near zero (|r| < 0.3) would support genre-difference explanation; positive correlation would falsify both and suggest a third structural factor

tests_run.test: for each section, compute the rank-frequency slope (zipf exponent) separately. if cipher-strength va

reasoning: If cipher strength varies by section, we expect a systematic trade-off between entropy and frequency concentration that should manifest as a strong negative correlation between section entropy and Zipf exponent â€” a prediction testable entirely from corpus statistics that would directly inform whether decipherment efforts should prioritize text-only/recipes (lighter cipher) or avoid diagrammatic sections (heavier cipher).
